title: Air.ai
text:
Case 2:25-cv-03068-SMB Document 1 Filed 08/25/25 Page 1 of 36
Rebecca Plett (VA Bar No. 90988, NC Bar No. 63273)
Lauren Rivard (NY Reg. No. 5308192)
Federal Trade Commission
600 Pennsylvania Ave., NW, Mailstop CC-6316
Washington, DC 20580
(202) 326-3664 / rplett@ftc.gov
(202) 326-2450 / lrivard@ftc.gov
Attorneys for Plaintiff
Federal Trade Commission
UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT
FOR THE DISTRICT OF ARIZONA
Federal Trade Commission,
Case No. _________________
Plaintiff,
COMPLAINT FOR PERMANENT
v. INJUNCTION, MONETARY
JUDGMENTS, AND OTHER
Air Ai Technologies, Inc., also d/b/a Air RELIEF
Ai and Scale 13, a Delaware corporation;
Apex Holdings Group LLC, an Arizona
limited liability company;
Apex Scaling LLC, also d/b/a Air AI and
Scale 13, an Arizona limited liability
company;
Apex 4 Kids LLC, an Arizona limited
liability company;
New Life Capital LLC, an Arizona limited
liability company;
Onyx Capital LLC, an Arizona limited
liability company;
Caleb Matthew Maddix, individually and
as an officer of Air Ai Technologies, Inc.,
Apex Holdings Group LLC, Apex Scaling
LLC, Apex 4 Kids LLC, New Life Capital
LLC, and Onyx Capital LLC;
Ryan Paul O’Donnell, individually and as
an officer of Air Ai Technologies, Inc.,
Apex Holdings Group LLC, Apex Scaling
LLC, Apex 4 Kids LLC, New Life Capital
LLC, and Onyx Capital LLC; and

Case 2:25-cv-03068-SMB Document 1 Filed 08/25/25 Page 2 of 36
1 Thomas Matthew Lancer, individually and
as an officer of Air Ai Technologies, Inc.,
2
Defendants.
3
4
Plaintiff, the Federal Trade Commission (“FTC” or “Commission”), for its
5
Complaint alleges:
6
1. The FTC brings this action for Defendants’ violations of Section 5(a) of the
7
8 FTC Act, 15 U.S.C. § 45(a), the Telemarketing Sales Rule, (“TSR”), 16 C.F.R. Part 310,
9
and the FTC’s Trade Regulation Rule entitled “Disclosure Requirements and Prohibitions
10
Concerning Business Opportunities” (“Business Opportunity Rule”), 16 C.F.R. Part 437,
11
as amended. For these violations, the FTC seeks relief, including a temporary,
12
13 preliminary, and permanent injunction, monetary relief, and other relief, pursuant to
14
Sections 13(b) and 19 of the FTC Act, 15 U.S.C. §§ 53(b), 57b, Section 6 of the
15
Telemarketing and Consumer Fraud and Abuse Prevention Act (“Telemarketing Act”),
16
17 15 U.S.C. § 6105, and the Business Opportunity Rule.
18
I. SUMMARY OF THE CASE
19
2. Since at least February 2023, Defendants have deceptively marketed and
20
sold a series of products and services aimed at entrepreneurs and small businesses,
21
cashing in on significant false earnings claims and a guaranteed refund policy, to bilk
22
23 consumers out of roughly $19 million over the course of only a few years.
24
3. Defendants use online and telephone advertising and sales funnels to reach
25
consumers, employing social media advertising campaigns to entice consumers to engage
26
27 with them over the phone. Once they have consumer contact information, Defendants
28
2

Case 2:25-cv-03068-SMB Document 1 Filed 08/25/25 Page 3 of 36
1 persistently call, text, and email consumers and use deceptive and high-pressure sales
2
tactics to sell their products and services.
3
4. Defendants’ products and services fall into two broad categories. First,
4
Defendants sell business coaching materials and support, coupled with a suite of AI-
5
6 related business support products called an “Air AI Access Card” membership.
7
Defendants have marketed their flagship product as “conversational AI,” allegedly
8
capable of replacing human customer service representatives and, in combination with
9
10 their coaching and other services, making business owners significant sums of money, for
11
example claiming that some consumers were making a million dollars using Defendants’
12
services or that consumers would earn back tens of thousands of dollars within 30 days.
13
5. Second, Defendants sell high-priced licenses permitting consumers to use
14
15 the conversational AI themselves and market and re-sell the conversational AI
16
technology to other businesses (“License” “Licensing” or “Licensing Business
17
Opportunity”). Some consumers purchasing a License enter the sales funnel looking to
18
purchase just conversational AI technology for their own business use but are then upsold
19
20 the “opportunity” to make considerably more money – recouping investments of up to
21
$100,000 and then some – by getting a License to resell the technology and get in on the
22
ground floor of this allegedly industry-shifting product.
23
6. Consumers purchasing the Air AI Access Card are frequently individuals
24
25 interested in starting coaching businesses, hoping that Defendants’ services will help
26
them start, grow, and scale their business, or entrepreneurs with pre-existing businesses
27
looking to incorporate AI to improve efficiency or expand their endeavors.
28
3

Case 2:25-cv-03068-SMB Document 1 Filed 08/25/25 Page 4 of 36
1 7. Despite Defendants’ promises, at best, Defendants offer coaching that does
2
not help consumers start or grow a business, glitchy software that does not perform as
3
advertised, and licenses to resell the same. At worst, Defendants sell consumers a junk
4
suite of services that do not exist or are not consistently available.
5
6 8. Consumers who purchase Air AI Access Cards often cannot get businesses
7
off the ground or bring in the promised profit, and licensees are not able to recoup the
8
money they pay Defendants for the privilege of selling their faulty products.
9
10 9. To induce consumers to purchase these products and services, Defendants
11
offer sweeping full-refund guarantees. These guarantees are the closing salvo of sales
12
pitches and expressly outlined in contracts. Consumers are promised that if they do not
13
make a certain amount of money – typically double or treble their investment within a
14
15 specified number of months – or if they are not happy with the products for any reason,
16
Defendants will refund the entire purchase price. As a result of these guarantees,
17
consumers enter contracts with Defendants believing that their investment is essentially
18
risk free. Some consumers even take on Defendant-brokered loans from third-party
19
20 lenders in order to cover the purchase price. But when consumers ask for a refund,
21
Defendants rarely honor their guarantee, often delaying and obfuscating before cutting
22
off communication altogether.
23
10. Consumers, many of whom are small business owners, lose as much as
24
25 $100,000 on their investment and are frequently left in debt after relying on Defendants’
26
false promises of business growth, earnings potential, and refunds.
27
28
4

Case 2:25-cv-03068-SMB Document 1 Filed 08/25/25 Page 5 of 36
1 II. JURISDICTION AND VENUE
2 11. This Court has subject matter jurisdiction pursuant to 28 U.S.C. §§ 1331,
3
1337(a), and 1345.
4
12. Venue is proper in this District under 28 U.S.C. §§ 1391(b)(2), (c)(1),
5
6 (c)(2), and 15 U.S.C. § 53(b).
7
III. PLAINTIFF
8
13. The FTC is an agency of the United States Government created by the FTC
9
Act, which authorizes the FTC to commence this district court civil action by its own
10
attorneys. 15 U.S.C. §§ 41–58. The FTC enforces Section 5(a) of the FTC Act, 15 U.S.C.
11
12 § 45(a), which prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce.
13
The FTC also enforces the Business Opportunity Rule, 16 C.F.R. Part 437, which
14
requires sellers to provide disclosure documents and prohibits false or unsubstantiated
15
earnings claims and other prohibited practices. In addition, the FTC enforces the
16
17
Telemarketing Act, 15 U.S.C. §§ 6101-6108. Pursuant to the Telemarketing Act, the FTC
18
promulgated and enforces the TSR, 16 C.F.R. Part 310, which prohibits deceptive and
19
abusive telemarketing acts or practices.
20
21 IV. DEFENDANTS
22 14. Defendant Air AI Technologies, Inc. (“Air AI”), also doing business as Air
23
AI, Air.AI and Scale 13, is a Delaware corporation with its principal place of business at
24
4742 N 24th Street, Suite 300, Phoenix, Arizona 85016. Air AI was previously
25
incorporated in Arizona as Air AI LLC, with its principal place of business at 4742 N
26
27 24th Street, Suite 300, Phoenix, Arizona 85016. In 2024, Air AI LLC converted to Air AI
28
Technologies, Inc. Air AI transacts or has transacted business in this District and
5

Case 2:25-cv-03068-SMB Document 1 Filed 08/25/25 Page 6 of 36
1 throughout the United States. At times relevant to this Complaint, acting alone or in
2
concert with others, Air AI has advertised, marketed, distributed, or sold the Air AI
3
Access Card and Licensing to consumers throughout the United States.
4
15. Defendant Apex Holdings Group LLC (“Apex Holdings”) is an Arizona
5
6 limited liability company with its principal place of business in Scottsdale, Arizona, with
7
a mailing address listed as 4742 N 24th Street, Suite 300, Phoenix, Arizona 85016. Apex
8
Holdings transacts or has transacted business in this District and throughout the United
9
10 States. At times relevant to this Complaint, acting alone or in concert with others, Apex
11
Holdings has advertised, marketed, distributed, or sold the Air AI Access Card and
12
Licensing to consumers throughout the United States. Apex Holdings is the principal and
13
organizer of corporate Defendant Apex Scaling LLC.
14
15 16. Defendant Apex Scaling LLC (“Apex Scaling”), also doing business as Air
16
AI and Scale 13, is an Arizona limited liability company with its principal place of
17
business in Scottsdale, Arizona, with a mailing address listed as 4742 N 24th Street, Suite
18
300, PMB #111, Phoenix, Arizona 85016. Apex Scaling transacts or has transacted
19
20 business in this District and throughout the United States. At times relevant to this
21
Complaint, acting alone or in concert with others, Apex Scaling has advertised, marketed,
22
distributed, or sold the Air Access Card and Licensing to consumers throughout the
23
United States.
24
25 17. Defendant Apex 4 Kids LLC (“Apex 4 Kids”) is an Arizona limited
26
liability company with its principal place of business in Scottsdale, Arizona, with a
27
mailing address listed as 4742 N 24th Street, Suite 300, PMB #111, Phoenix, Arizona
28
6

Case 2:25-cv-03068-SMB Document 1 Filed 08/25/25 Page 7 of 36
1 85016. Apex 4 Kids transacts or has transacted business in this District and throughout
2
the United States. At times relevant to this Complaint, acting alone or in concert with
3
others, Apex 4 Kids has advertised, marketed, distributed, or sold the Air AI Access Card
4
and Licensing to consumers throughout the United States.
5
6 18. Defendant New Life Capital LLC (“New Life Capital”) is an Arizona
7
limited liability company with its principal place of business at 4742 N 24th Street, Suite
8
300, Phoenix, Arizona 85016 with a mailing address listed as PO Box 111, Phoenix,
9
10 Arizona 85016. New Life Capital transacts or has transacted business in this District and
11
throughout the United States. At times relevant to this Complaint, acting alone or in
12
concert with others, New Life Capital has advertised, marketed, distributed, or sold the
13
Air AI Access Card and Licensing to consumers throughout the United States.
14
15 19. Defendant Onyx Capital LLC (“Onyx Capital”) is an Arizona limited
16
liability company with its principal place of business at 4742 N 24th Street, Suite 300,
17
Phoenix, Arizona 85016 with a mailing address listed as PO Box 111, Phoenix, Arizona
18
85016. Onyx Capital transacts or has transacted business in this District and throughout
19
20 the United States. At times relevant to this Complaint, acting alone or in concert with
21
others, Onyx Capital has advertised, marketed, distributed, or sold the Air AI Access
22
Card and Licensing to consumers throughout the United States.
23
20. Defendant Caleb Matthew Maddix (“Defendant Maddix”) is a managing
24
25 member of Air AI, Apex Holdings, Apex Scaling, Apex 4 Kids, New Life Capital, and
26
Onyx Capital (“Corporate Defendants”). At all times relevant to this Complaint, acting
27
alone or in concert with others, he has formulated, directed, controlled, had the authority
28
7

Case 2:25-cv-03068-SMB Document 1 Filed 08/25/25 Page 8 of 36
1 to control, or participated in the acts and practices of advertising, marketing, distributing,
2
or selling the Air AI Access Card and Licensing to consumers throughout the United
3
States, including the acts and practices described in this Complaint.
4
21. Defendant Maddix resides in Maricopa County, Arizona, and in connection
5
6 with the matters alleged herein, transacts or has transacted business in this District and
7
throughout the United States.
8
22. Defendant Ryan Paul O’Donnell (“Defendant O’Donnell”) is a managing
9
10 member of the Corporate Defendants. At all times relevant to this Complaint, acting
11
alone or in concert with others, he has formulated, directed, controlled, had the authority
12
to control, or participated in the acts and practices of advertising, marketing, distributing,
13
or selling the Air AI Access Card and Licensing to consumers throughout the United
14
15 States, including the acts and practices described in this Complaint.
16
23. Defendant O’Donnell resides in Fairfax County, Virginia, and in
17
connection with the matters alleged herein, transacts or has transacted business in this
18
District and throughout the United States.
19
20 24. Defendant Thomas Matthew Lancer (“Defendant Lancer”) is a principal of
21
Air AI. At all times relevant to this Complaint, acting alone or in concert with others, he
22
has formulated, directed, controlled, had the authority to control, or participated in the
23
acts and practices of advertising, marketing, distributing, or selling the Air AI Access
24
25 Card and Licensing to consumers throughout the United States, including the acts and
26
practices described in this Complaint.
27
25. Defendant Lancer resides in Canyon County, Idaho, and in connection with
28
8

Case 2:25-cv-03068-SMB Document 1 Filed 08/25/25 Page 9 of 36
1 the matters alleged herein, transacts or has transacted business in this District and
2
throughout the United States.
3
V. COMMON ENTERPRISE
4
26. Corporate Defendants have operated as a common enterprise while
5
6 engaging in the deceptive and unlawful acts and practices and other violations of law
7
alleged below. Corporate Defendants have conducted the business practices described
8
below through an interrelated network of companies that have common ownership, office
9
locations, and commingled funds.
10
11 27. Defendants Maddix and O’Donnell are principals for all six Corporate
12
Defendants and are sole principals for Apex Holdings, Apex 4 Kids, and New Life
13
Capital. They have signatory authority on corporate bank accounts for all Corporate
14
Defendants. Defendants Maddix, O’Donnell and Lancer (“Individual Defendants”) are
15
16 principals of Air AI, and Defendant Lancer has access to corporate credit cards for Air AI
17
and Apex 4 Kids credit card accounts.
18
28. All Corporate Defendants have shared at least one address, which they list
19
in articles of incorporation as a principal place of business or mailing address: 4742 N
20
21 24th Street, Suite 300, Phoenix, Arizona 85016. This address appears to be a co-working
22
space offering monthly memberships, meeting rooms for reservation, and virtual offices.
23
29. Certain Corporate Defendants also share employees. For example, at least
24
25 one employee listed as a contractor for Air AI received payments from a bank account
26
associated with Apex Scaling, had access to and made payments from a bank account
27
associated with Apex 4 Kids, and had some Apex 4 Kids bank statements mailed to their
28
9

Case 2:25-cv-03068-SMB Document 1 Filed 08/25/25 Page 10 of 36
1 home address. Moreover, Apex Holdings and Air AI shared at least a dozen
2
“contractors.”
3
30. In addition, Corporate Defendants engage in regular transfers of revenue
4
between the common enterprise members, comingling over $23,631,000 between the
5
6 corporate bank accounts. For example, a bank account for Air AI transferred nearly $14
7
million to bank accounts for each Corporate Defendant and received funds from bank
8
accounts associated with Apex Holdings, Apex Scaling, New Life Capital, and Onyx
9
10 Capital.
11
31. Because these Corporate Defendants, under the Individual Defendants’
12
direction, have operated as a common enterprise, each of them is liable for the acts and
13
practices alleged below.
14
15 VI. COMMERCE
16 32. At all times relevant to this Complaint, Defendants have maintained a
17
substantial course of trade in or affecting commerce, as “commerce” is defined in Section
18
4 of the FTC Act, 15 U.S.C. § 44.
19
20 VII. DEFENDANTS’ BUSINESS ACTIVITIES
21 A. Defendants’ Products and Services
22 33. Defendants market and sell a set of goods and services (collectively
23
“Services”): (1) a bundle of coaching and business education services, (2) a suite of
24
business support software, and (3) resale Licensing for their software for sale to the
25
public.
26
27 34. Defendants typically refer to the coaching services and business support
28
software collectively as the “Air AI Access Card” (or, sometimes, the “Air AI Program”),
10

Case 2:25-cv-03068-SMB Document 1 Filed 08/25/25 Page 11 of 36
1 which they sell for prices ranging from $15,000 to $30,000. Licensing, which includes
2
some coaching services, typically costs even more, with prices ranging from $25,000 to
3
$100,000.
4
35. Defendants started out selling the Air AI Access Card and related coaching
5
6 services. But when they were unable to deliver the services they advertised, they also
7
began marketing just the conversational AI (without some of the features included with
8
the Air AI Access Card), as well as the Licensing Business Opportunity.
9
10 i. Coaching Services
11 36. Defendants claim that by using their coaching and other services,
12
consumers will be able to make double or more what they spend purchasing Air AI in as
13
little as three months, upward of tens of thousands of dollars. If not, Defendants promise
14
a full refund. Defendants also claim that consumers have the potential of earning
15
16 millions.
17
37. Defendants assert that Air AI’s coaching program, at times referred to as
18
“Scale 13,” will help consumers develop and market their own online coaching business.
19
38. According to Defendants, their Air AI Access Card and Licensing Business
20
21 Opportunity each provide access to a number of educational materials and promotional
22
services, including the “Air AI training platform,” which encompasses “a library of
23
internal processes and systems for operating and scaling a high-ticket business,” “[a]ccess
24
25 to Air University 2.0, Air’s step-by-step guide to building and scaling systems,” “live
26
group consulting calls with AIR AI’s internal team,” “live sales training with Air Ai’s
27
sales team for you or your team,” and various other marketing and sales resources.
28
11

Case 2:25-cv-03068-SMB Document 1 Filed 08/25/25 Page 12 of 36
1 39. Defendants promise “soup-to-nuts” training and support, such as a large
2
team of dedicated tech representatives for each individual customer through a dedicated
3
messaging platform channel, top-to-bottom support to build consumers’ coaching
4
courses, and daily one-on-one coaching with the founders of Air AI.
5
6 40. In reality, Defendants’ promised educational materials and support fall
7
significantly short of the claims made in their marketing. Instead of the one-on-one
8
coaching promised, consumers can access only group coaching calls where they have
9
10 little opportunity to ask questions or receive individualized support. When questions are
11
addressed, the answers do not typically solve problems, forcing consumers to waste their
12
time attending more group calls.
13
41. And while Defendants’ sales pitch claims consumers will receive coaching
14
15 and technical assistance in dedicated messaging platform channels, Defendants often do
16
not answer questions in those channels in a timely manner, if at all. As with the group
17
coaching calls, when Defendants do respond, the advice and guidance that is provided
18
frequently does not resolve consumer issues, making it difficult for consumers to use the
19
20 Services they purchased.
21
42. In part because they lacked critical guidance and in part because at least
22
some consumers were required to finish one module before being permitted to view the
23
next, some consumers found it impossible to complete the coaching program, let alone
24
25 launch a business and bring in the revenue promised within the timeframes outlined in
26
their contracts. There was no way to earn back their investment within three or six
27
months – as promised by their money back guarantees – because it was exceedingly
28
12

Case 2:25-cv-03068-SMB Document 1 Filed 08/25/25 Page 13 of 36
1 difficult to progress through the program that quickly, or at all.
2
ii. Software Suite
3
43. Defendants’ software suite, offered as part of the Air AI Access Card,
4
professes to include four central features: (1) a “[l]ead maximizer and dialing platform”
5
6 called “Max” that can purportedly autodial phone numbers for Air AI Access Card
7
holders; (2) a “checkout and financing platform” called “Midas,” meant to connect the
8
Air AI Access Card holder’s future customers with payment and financing options; (3) a
9
10 purported “[a]nalytics platform and constraint finder” called “Sherlock”; and (4) a
11
conversational AI technology sometimes called “Odin.”
12
44. “Max” is a software platform that automatically dials phone numbers to
13
place outbound calls.
14
15 45. “Midas” acts as a checkout platform and loan lead generator and was
16
supposed to match consumers’ future customers with loans from third-party lenders.
17
46. Consumers were also promised access to or first notice upon the release of
18
the “Sherlock” analytics tool, but the feature never materialized.
19
20 47. Much of Defendants’ advertising focused on the conversational AI
21
technology sometimes called “Odin,” claiming it would save consumers time, money,
22
and effort by replacing existing or future sales representatives with AI agents.
23
48. According to Defendants, their conversational AI technology can “have full
24
25 on 10-40 minute long phone conversations that sound like a REAL human, with infinite
26
memory, perfect recall, and can autonomously take actions across 5,000 plus
27
applications” and “can do the entire job of a full time agent without having to be trained,
28
13

Case 2:25-cv-03068-SMB Document 1 Filed 08/25/25 Page 14 of 36
1 managed or motivated.” Access, or future access, to this feature is included in the Air AI
2
Access Card and is marketed as the core feature of the Licensing Business Opportunity.
3
Defendants charge consumers by the minute for using the conversational AI technology.
4
49. Defendants claim that their conversational AI is better than human sales
5
6 representatives because it has “[z]ero ramp up time and can deliver above average
7
performance out of the gate” and “requires zero management.” For example, in a
8
marketing video, Defendants display the following screen highlighting the claimed
9
10 efficiencies created by using AI instead of a human representative, including that AI
11
“never fails to deliver consistent performance,” “[g]ets less expensive every year (yet
12
better everyday [sic]),” and works “24/7.”
13
14
/fir
15 Average Rep
16 X Clocks in and clocks out / takes vacations ✓ Works 24/7 and never takes time off
X Manual hiring process that requires time ✓ Can instantly hire 100,000 reps at the tap of a button
17
X Takes 3.2 months to ramp up and hit KPI' ✓ Zero ramp up time and can deliver above average
performance out of the gate
18 X Burnout/ lack of motivation/ inconsistency in
performance ✓ Never burns out, always has great energy/attitude, and
never fails to deliver consistent performance
19 X Requires training and management (and with that a
management team) ✓ Is constantly learning/training on it's own and requires
zero management
20 X Requires HR liability/ paying for benefits/ payroll
taxes etc ✓ Zero HR liability and doesn't require you to pay for
benefits or payroll taxes
21 X More expensive every year when you factor in raises
✓ Gets less expensive every year (yet better everyday)
X Have to deal with churn & focus on retention. The
22 average rep only stays at a company 1.5 years' ✓ Zero churn & eliminates need to focus on retention
X Costs $115,000 to replace 1 sales rep' ✓ Because there's no churn you never have to pay to
23 replace reps
24
1: bttas·//bloa btthsoor cam/sales/how·to-maoaae-a·hiah-aectorroina-sales·team.
2: httos·/Jmeetmaestro com/bloas/the-cost-of-reolacina-a-sales-rep/
25
50. But Defendants’ sweeping statements and representations about the
26
capabilities of their conversational AI are overblown. As an initial matter, for some Air
27
28 AI Access Card holders, the conversational AI technology was never available, even
14

Case 2:25-cv-03068-SMB Document 1 Filed 08/25/25 Page 15 of 36
1 though Defendants promised to give these consumers “first notice” upon releasing the
2
feature.
3
51. And even when available, the conversational AI technology does not
4
function as advertised. Instead, the technology is faulty, often not able to perform basic
5
6 functions like placing outbound calls to businesses, scheduling appointments, taking
7
down email addresses, or responding accurately to questions.
8
52. Rather than a built-for-you solution that requires minimal effort, getting the
9
10 conversational AI to function at all requires a substantial time commitment where
11
consumers must pre-script answers for every potential question, making it nearly
12
impossible to use.
13
iii. Licensing Business Opportunity
14
53. In addition to selling the Air AI Access Card, Defendants also offer a
15
16 business opportunity that provides consumers with the option to purchase a License to
17
use and resell “the world’s most sophisticated conversational AI” to other businesses.
18
The Licensing Business Opportunity is supposed to include coaching, as well as support
19
services, notably “a thousand pre vetted, qualified business owner leads.”
20
21 54. Defendants claim that consumers can earn millions of dollars in income by
22
referring and reselling their conversational AI technology to others, including by
23
receiving a percentage of the revenue Defendants earn from AI calls placed by referred
24
25 consumers. For example, Defendants claim that License holders could bring in a single
26
business and get paid one million dollars a month, and that “we’ve organically had
27
companies reach out to us that have over a hundred thousand reps. So if you had a rep
28
15

Case 2:25-cv-03068-SMB Document 1 Filed 08/25/25 Page 16 of 36
1 like that, that’d be like ten million dollars a month paid out to you.”
2
55. Defendants also claim that, because their conversational AI technology is
3
both demonstrably and logically better than human sales representatives, it will be easy
4
for consumers to resell because “The Easiest Thing on the Planet To Distribute Is a
5
6 Product That is Cheaper Than the Alternative & Performs Exponentially Better.”
7
However, due to the poor performance and quality of the conversational AI technology,
8
many consumers who purchase the Licensing Business Opportunity are not able to resell
9
10 it to anyone.
11
56. For example, in an attempt to promote the conversational AI to a building
12
company, one License-holding consumer tried for months to build out an AI test agent
13
that could answer questions about a few different housing floor plans and schedule
14
15 potential customers for follow-up meetings. After troubleshooting and working around
16
numerous software flaws – including the AI agent reading portions of the script not
17
intended to be said aloud, hanging up on consumers moments after they picked up the
18
phone, and struggling to accurately transcribe even basic consumer information – the
19
20 License-holding consumer found that the AI test agent would not stick to the script, and
21
simply made-up answers when it was asked questions for which he had not supplied
22
responses. He determined that he could not possibly present the product to the intended
23
buyer because it would not work as advertised.
24
25
26
27
28
16

Case 2:25-cv-03068-SMB Document 1 Filed 08/25/25 Page 17 of 36
1 B. Defendants’ Deceptive and High-Pressure Sales Funnel for Air Access Card and
2 Licensing Business Opportunity
3
57. Defendants’ sales funnel relies on a variety of misrepresentations –
4
including earnings claims misrepresentations and misrepresentations related to refund
5
guarantees.
6
7 58. Defendants’ sales funnel consists of online and social media advertising,
8
telephone calls, video presentations, text messages, and emails.
9
59. Since at least 2023, Defendants have disseminated, or caused to be
10
disseminated, advertising for their Services, beginning with ads related to the Air AI
11
12 Access Card, including through social media ads, online videos, and their website.
13
60. Some of Defendants’ online advertising has been focused on the business
14
coaching element of the Air AI Access Card, promising that Defendants’ Services will
15
help consumers develop, start, or grow a business. According to a consumer, one ad
16
17
claimed that Defendants could rapidly triple a company’s income.
18
61. Defendants also advertise their Licensing Business Opportunity. For
19
example, some of Defendants’ advertising for a “VIP” edition of their Licensing included
20

16 IX. VIOLATIONS OF THE TELEMARKETING SALES RULE REGARDING
17 DEFENDANTS’ SALE OF AIR AI ACCESS CARDS
18 111. In 1994, Congress directed the FTC to prescribe rules prohibiting abusive
19
and deceptive telemarketing acts or practices pursuant to the Telemarketing Act, 15
20
U.S.C. §§ 6101–6108. The FTC adopted the original TSR in 1995, extensively amended
21
it in 2003, and amended certain sections thereafter.
22
23 112. Defendants are “seller[s]” or “telemarketer[s]” engaging in “telemarketing”
24
as defined by the TSR, 16 C.F.R. § 310.2(ee), (hh), and (ii). The TSR defines
25
telemarketers as “any person who, in connection with telemarketing, initiates or receives
26
27 telephone calls to or from a customer or donor.” 16 C.F.R. § 310.2(hh). The TSR defines
28
“telemarketing” as “a plan, program, or campaign which is conducted to induce the
29

Case 2:25-cv-03068-SMB Document 1 Filed 08/25/25 Page 30 of 36
1 purchase of goods or services or a charitable contribution, by use of one or more
2
telephones and which involves more than one interstate telephone call.” 16 C.F.R.
3
§ 310.2(ii). A “seller” under the TSR is “any person who, in connection with a
4
telemarketing transaction, provides, offers to provide, or arranges for others to provide
5
6 goods or services to the customer in exchange for consideration.” 16 C.F.R. § 310.2(ee).
7
113. As alleged in Section VII.A. and B., Defendants initiated and received
8
telephone calls to and from customers in connection with their plan or campaign to
9
10 induce the purchase of their Services, including through interstate telephone calls. As part
11
of their plan or campaign, they offered to provide their Services to customers in exchange
12
for consideration.
13
114. The TSR generally exempts certain inbound telephone calls from coverage
14
15 under the Rule. See, e.g., 16 C.F.R. § 310.6(b)(5). However, inbound calls related to the
16
sale of investment opportunities or that involve upsells are not exempt from the TSR. 16
17
C.F.R. § 310.6(b)(5)(i) and (ii). Under the TSR, an investment opportunity “means
18
anything, tangible or intangible, that is offered, offered for sale, sold, or traded based
19
20 wholly or in part on representations, either express or implied, about past, present, or
21
future income, profit, or appreciation.” 16 C.F.R. § 310.2(s)
22
115. Defendants make outbound telemarketing calls to consumers. They also
23
engage in the sale of investment opportunities when they make express or implied
24
25 representations about future income or profit that their customers will be able to realize
26
by purchasing Defendants’ Services. In some instances, Defendants also upsell customers
27
to their Licensing.
28
30

5
6 DEFENDANTS’ SALE OF LICENSING BUSINESS OPPORTUNITIES
7 121. The amended Business Opportunity Rule, 16 C.F.R. Part 437, which was
8
extended in scope to cover certain work-at-home opportunities, became effective on
9
March 1, 2012.
10
122. Defendants are “sellers” who, as described in Section VII.A. and B., have
11
12 sold or offered to sell “business opportunities” as defined by the Business Opportunity
13
Rule, 16 C.F.R. § 437.1(c) and (q).
14
123. Under the Business Opportunity Rule, a “seller” is a person who offers for
15
sale or sells a business opportunity. 16 C.F.R. § 437.1(q). Defendants offer to sell and sell
16
17
business opportunities to consumers.
18
124. Under the Rule, a “business opportunity” means a “commercial
19
arrangement” in which a “seller solicits a prospective purchaser to enter into a new
20
21 business;” the “prospective purchaser makes a required payment;” and the “seller,
22
expressly or by implication, orally or in writing, represents that the seller or one or more
23
designated persons will … [p]rovide outlets, accounts, or customers, including, but not
24
limited to, Internet outlets, accounts, or customers, for the purchaser’s goods or
25
26 services[.]” 16 C.F.R. § 437.1(c). Defendants sell consumers “business opportunities”
27
when they sell their Licensing by soliciting consumers to enter into the business of
28
reselling Defendants’ software and promising consumers leads.
32

Case 2:25-cv-03068-SMB Document 1 Filed 08/25/25 Page 33 of 36
1 125. Among other things, the Business Opportunity Rule requires sellers to
2
provide prospective purchasers with a disclosure document in the form and using the
3
language set forth in the Business Opportunity Rule and its Appendix A, and any
4
required attachments. In the disclosure document, the seller must disclose to prospective
5
6 purchasers five categories of information, including: basic identifying information about
7
the seller, any earnings claims the seller makes, the seller’s litigation history, any
8
cancellation or refund policy the seller offers, and contact information of prior
9
10 purchasers. 16 C.F.R. § 437.3(a)(1)–(5). Furthermore, this information must be disclosed
11
at least seven (7) days before the prospective purchaser signs a contract or makes a
12
payment. 16 C.F.R. § 437.2.
13
126. The pre-sale disclosure of this information enables a prospective purchaser
14
15 to contact prior purchasers and take other steps to assess the potential risks involved in
16
the purchase of the business opportunity.
17
127. Defendants do not provide consumers with disclosure documents as
18
required by the Business Opportunity Rule.
19
20 128. Defendants, as described in Paragraph 73(A)-(J), have made earnings
21
claims in connection with the sale of their business opportunities, as defined by the
22
Business Opportunity Rule, 16 C.F.R. § 437.1(f). Under the Business Opportunity Rule,
23
an “earnings claim” means “any oral, written, or visual representation to a prospective
24
25 purchaser that conveys, expressly or by implication, a specific level or range of actual or
26
potential sales, or gross or net income or profits.” 16 C.F.R. § 437.1(f).
27
28
33

Case 2:25-cv-03068-SMB Document 1 Filed 08/25/25 Page 34 of 36
1 129. The Business Opportunity Rule prohibits sellers from making earnings
2
claims unless the seller: (1) has a reasonable basis for the claim at the time it is made;
3
(2) has in its possession written materials to substantiate the claim at the time it is made;
4
(3) furnishes an earnings claim statement to prospective purchasers in conjunction with
5
6 the disclosure document, containing, among other things, information regarding the time
7
frame captured by the earnings claim, the characteristics of the purchasers, and the
8
number and percentage of all persons who purchased the business opportunity within the
9
10 time frame who achieved at least the stated level of earnings; and (4) makes written
11
substantiation of the earnings claim available to any prospective purchaser who requests
12
it. 16 C.F.R. § 437.4(a).
13
130. Defendants do not furnish earnings claims statements to customers.
14
15 131. The Business Opportunity Rule also prohibits sellers from making certain
16
misrepresentations, including about (1) the amount of sales, or gross or net income or
17
profits a prospective purchaser may earn; and (2) any term or condition of the seller’s
18
refund or cancellation policies. 16 C.F.R. § 437.6(d), (i), (k). Sellers are also prohibited
19
20 from failing to provide refunds or cancellations when consumers meet the relevant terms
21
and conditions. 16 C.F.R. § 437.6(l).
22
132. Defendants, as described in Section VII.C., have misrepresented the
23
amount of income or profits consumers may earn and their refund policies. They have

25 also failed to refund consumers who met the refund guarantee criteria.

5
6 Business Opportunities)

(i), (k) and (l), and Section 5(a) of the FTC Act, 15 U.S.C. § 45(a).
